authorities.name: California Department of Fish and Wildlife

sources.title: CCR T14 §5.85(a)(1) — Trout, General Statewide Regulations (inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds)
sources.quotedText: All inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds, including those on private lands, are open to fishing all year with a five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit.

sources.title: CCR T14 §7.40 — Alphabetical List of Hatchery Trout, Hatchery Steelhead, and Salmon Waters with Special Fishing Regulations (Horseshoe Lake, El Dorado Co., not listed)

sources.quotedText: (60) Horseshoe Lake (Mono Co.). Last Sat. in Apr. through Nov. 15. 5 trout

sources.title: 2026 California Freshwater Sport Fishing Regulations (Updated 06/01/2026)
sources.quotedText: All inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds, including those on private lands, are open to fishing all year with a five-trout daily bag limit and 10 trout possession limit.

sources.title: California Fish and Game Code §7145 — Sport Fishing License Requirement
sources.quotedText: Except as otherwise provided in this article, every person 16 years of age or older who takes any fish, reptile, or amphibian for any purpose other than profit shall first obtain a valid license for that purpose...

sources.title: 2026 Provisional Stocking Document for Trout and Inland Salmon — CDFW (Desolation Wilderness Horseshoe Lake, El Dorado Co., not listed among release waters; the listed 'Horseshoe Lake' is the Region 2 Alameda Co. put-and-take lake)
CDFW Memorandum (23 March 2021) — Native amphibian monitoring in Desolation Wilderness; Rana sierrae monitoring at Lake Zitella (High Mountain Lakes, North Central Region Fisheries)
sources.quotedText: The Aquatic Biodiversity Management Plan (ABMP) for the Desolation Wilderness Management Unit identifies Lake Zitella, Horseshoe Lake, McConnell Lake, Leland Lakes, and 4-Q Lakes as a Native Species Reserve (NSR) for SNYLF (CDFG 2012).

sources.title: USGS GNIS (The National Map Gazetteer) — Lake layer record for gaz_id 225647, "Horseshoe Lake", Lake, El Dorado County, CA
sources.quotedText: {"gaz_id":225647,"gaz_name":"Horseshoe Lake","gaz_featureclass":"Lake","fcode":39004,"state_alpha":"CA","county_name":"El Dorado"}; geometry (Web Mercator -13382995.0516, 4715097.9335) converts to lon -120.221490, lat 38.954093, which is ~14m from the file's recorded coordinate (-120.22145, 38.95397) — within tolerance, coordinate confirmed.

parameters.note: Horseshoe Lake (mapped by OpenStreetMap as 'Horsehoe Lake') is a high alpine lake in the northwest corner of the Desolation Wilderness (El Dorado Co.), immediately southeast of Lake Zitella in the upper Phipps Creek planning watershed. Despite sitting just west of the Sierra crest near Lake Tahoe, it is NOT in the Lake Tahoe basin: this headwater system drains west into the Rubicon River, which flows to Rubicon Reservoir and on to the Middle Fork American River — the American River watershed — so the Lake Tahoe tributary closure (§7.50(b)(152)) has no application here. The lake is not listed by name in §7.40(b) or §7.50(b) (the only 'Horseshoe Lake' in §7.50(b) is the unrelated Mono Co. lake at (60)), so it falls under the general statewide lake regulation §5.85(a)(1): open all year, 5/10. Fish status: CDFW's Aquatic Biodiversity Management Plan designates Lake Zitella, Horseshoe Lake, McConnell Lake, Leland Lakes, and the 4-Q Lakes as a Native Species Reserve for the Sierra Nevada yellow-legged frog (Rana sierrae); these steep granitic headwater lakes are managed as native-amphibian habitat and are largely fishless, and Horseshoe Lake is not in CDFW's 2026 Provisional Stocking Document. No current authoritative source names a fish population here, so species and stocking are intentionally left empty; the season/bag/license rows below state what governs the lake body under general regulation, independent of whether fish are present.

regulations.humanSummary: Open all year (general statewide lake regulation; not listed as a special-regulation water). Note: in the American/Rubicon River drainage (not the Lake Tahoe basin) and managed as a fishless Native Species Reserve for the Sierra Nevada yellow-legged frog.
regulations.verbatimText: All inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds, including those on private lands, are open to fishing all year with a five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit.

parameters.counting_rule: General statewide trout limit for inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds: five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit.
